review_text: intro: insanely passionate professor that is always working on the class and how it works, pretty cool in general pros: - open note exams - fun lecturer that records lectures (i mainly had to watch but he is good) - good on answering questions, being available to students, even goes on piazza every now and then - gradescope homeworks that are quizzes, can retake - labs are useful to content - projects are fair, mostly public tests then manual inspection - extra credit available based on class engagement and some other small factors which helps with grades - constantly looks for feedback through surveys - piazza is good, incentive to discuss and engage with each other, prof will be on there at times - besides recordings/slides/example code, other things are also posted and are available for use - practice exam reviews a bit of the content, it is reviewed in lecture before exam (good overall) - makeup credit available on projects in a way - TAs are pretty helpful neutral - flow of class/content/lectures expects you to be on top of work constantly rather than doing it last minute including him asking questions in lecture to class - "required" textbook, available online when I took class, reading it is not actually necessary but it seems like very good supplemental material - discussion section not necessary to attend (when I took class) but offers good reinforcement on labs and content cons - one part of labs is a quiz section but you don't know what questions are right or wrong when you check the answers, so sometimes its hard to know what you did wrong and end up wasting extra time conclusion: take the class! he's a good professor

Was falsely accused by Kauffman last semester. Completely wrecked my relationship with UMD CS imo. He published his own statistics on AI cheating in his class which showed that at least 19.6% of the class was penalized with a grade reduction or a pending XF case (XF is an academic integrity mark which removes CS students from the major.) Even more students than that were accused. On one project, he mass accused and penalized 15% of the class for cheating using compiler-generated code or AI because they used an "advanced" command, even though it was listed in the class slides, project rubric, project description, textbook, and an approved linked webpage on his course site. He also said we could reference compiler-generated code for the project and even bring AI-generated notes and content to exams, which makes it really unclear about what’s actually allowed. He originally accused even more students for the same issue until realizing he had accidentally included a non-project-related file in his checker. He said he had high confidence in the accusation, and offered a 0 to those accused on the project (~2% overall grade reduction) or a one-on-one interview process where success would lead to a regrade request and failure results in a XF recommendation to OSC. Over 1/3 of the accused students still showed up despite the existential threat of taking the interview, which makes me believe that he was really throwing around accusations. Either way, Kauffman later wrote in a post that he "was never once convinced of a student's innocence during an interview" and those who were let go only had "plausible deniability." The process was extremely stressful and lacked transparency. At the end of his course, 20 students in his section went to OSC. For context, there were 50 AI-related OSC across all departments and classes at UMD for the entire year of 2023, meaning his section last semester would account for around half of that. Whether you believe it or not, I know many people who were accused, didn't cheat, and were penalized anyway, including myself. His passive aggressive comments about not giving extra credit on later projects because of the cheating cases and verbal/written rants about "cheating" students devaluing the CS degree before any of the cases were even resolved made the class feel toxic, distrustful, and stressful like everyone was constantly under suspicion. On top of the difficult content, the threat of being accused and kicked out of the major over one or two lines of code on a project that is flagged as suspicious could seriously impact anyone’s mental health. He may be allowing AI in future courses to avoid this issue again (or in his words, because too many people are "cheating"), but this is my two cents. 2 stars because I learned a lot in his class as mentioned by everyone else below me.

Great professor. While he can be a bit critical and passive aggressive, he does an outstanding job at actually teaching the course content. Each topic is explained in great detail, there is a ton of resources provided outside of lecture to study and review. The course itself is also quite fun, but can be challenging. Also open note midterms and final is pretty great. Highly recommend taking him whenever possible.

Amazing professor! All lectures are recorded, exams are graded fairly, labs encourage group work and actual learning. Rewards effort in the class through small extra credit/make up credit which can help your grade if you're 1 point away from the next letter. I would highly recommend him.

Kauffman and I had our enemies to lovers arc this semester. At the beginning of the semester with the stuff introducing C, I felt he went too slow, I felt the labs were unhelpful, and I felt bored in the class. But once Kauffman got to Assembly, and especially once he got to the UNIX and hardware material at the end of the semester, he really opened my eyes to learning about computer systems in general. Professor Kauffman has an enthusiastic lecturing style---you can tell he cares a lot about our understanding of the material, and the labs and homeworks he provides really do give great practice for his exams. His exams are also challenging, but definitely fair, and the open resources were a nice touch that I genuinely used, albeit barely, but when I used it it was absolutely needed. There was never a question I did not know how to do on his exams given the practice material provides you with exact question types you might see. Professor Kauffman has a plethora of pop culture references as well which keep him feeling simultaneously like your old uncle and someone in the know. I would recommend anyone take Professor Kauffman if you value your learning and your education more than your grade. His projects are difficult, but manageable if you start early, and I never had problems submitting on time nor with the actual coding itself. If you put in the work, you will get the A you desire. I have to dock one star because he called my supreme overlord Larry Herman "Professor" Herman. Everyone knows the GOAT is an instructor, NOT a professor.

Amazing Professor! Lectures are very engaging and he explains things clearly. Grading is very fair and he gives lots of opportunities for bonus/make up credit. Labs and HWs are relatively easy and focus on introducing concepts. Projects are hard but not impossible and they truly teach you the material. Exams are fairly difficult but they do reflect what's taught in class and are similar to the practice exams given. If you study hard and understand the labs and projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. Re doing labs on paper or parts of projects is a great way to study, the exams usually have pretty normal coding questions with the rest being short answer. The short answer matters just as much as the coding in terms of grading which isn't bad by any means. He gives extra credit for participating in lectures and answering questions on piazza which is a great idea!! I initially was motivated to participate for the extra credit but after the first few weeks of class, I found myself participating out of genuine curiosity or just wanting to engage with the material. For piazza, this was the one class where students would always respond to questions quickly which was very helpful. Overall, Kauffman is a great professor and I'm glad I got to take this class with him!

pros: smart dude, good demonstrations, worksheets,projects,participation is grade padding, and final exam questions are very similar to the midterm and practice exams. cons: no phones no laptops during lecture, he wastes probably 5 minutes each class stuttering or calling people out for using their phone. unforgiving n strict, slow at grading, he gatekeeps online communication and office hours behind a fat block of text and rules u gotta read (this one not that bad just annoying). multiple choice section on exams is notoriously difficult and specific, he also DOCKS POINTS FOR WRONG ANSWERS which is apparently to "discourage guessing" but inherently makes every second guess themself lol. we had a failing midterm average. lectures are also incredibly boring but thats expected for any cs class ig. he did curve final grades idk what the dude 2 below is sayin looking back i would take an 8am to avoid larry in a heartbeat but thats jus me

I'd like to start off by saying that he is not inherently the worst of the worst, I think that his approach at teaching is not the best, but I don't think he means it with ill-intent. I barely scraped by and ended with a 73% which resulted in a C, even after always scoring below the average on all his exams and quizzes. The way that the course was structured, at least when I took his class, was set up in a way that the projects and exercises made up a substantial part of the overall course grade. Lab works also made up a small but considerable percentage that can boost your grade by the time finals come around. If I am not mistaken at the beginning of the course, he acknowledged that his exams and quizzes were known to be difficult, but that as long as you completed the projects and exercises on time and tried your best in exams and quizzes you were going to be okay. Projects: His projects were not inherently difficult, they were at times confusing, but attending office hours and posting on Piazza ultimately cleared any confusions you may have had. Exercises could often be done in a day, but I would suggest not doing this for both exercises and projects. Lab works were also easy to complete, and we were given enough time to complete them. Just by completing these on time, you can be at a good standing before exams and quizzes tank you grade (and they will, unless you are the type to ruin the curve.) Exams/Quizzes: His exams and quizzes were confusing, tedious, and soul draining. Often times it was never truly clear what exactly we were going to be tested on. Before exams or quizzes, he would send out emails containing a list of what we had covered since the previous quiz/exam, and he would say that what was on the slides was testable material, so it would be hard to differentiate what exactly was going to be on the test/quiz. On one occasion, what he stated was going to be covered on a quiz were assembly function calls and parent-child processes; given the difficulty and notoriety of his quizzes I studied everything assembly and assembly-function call related, and only studied parent-child processes some half hour before the quiz and ultimately the quiz only contained one assembly multiple choice question and the rest of the quiz was all related to parent-child processes; this time the quiz was easier than expected, but the angst and confusion leading up to the quiz takes a toll on you. Part of the shortcomings related to quizzes and exams *could* be attributed to a student's lack of complete familiarity and understanding of the material, however, unless the only thing you have going for you is school (and that is okay, great actually, if it's the case for you - it is not often, rarely actually, for that to be the case for everyone) then it is very difficult to develop mastery of the material in time before exams and quizzes. Some topics may be covered a lecture or two before an exam/quiz and some of the questions on quizzes and exams feel like they are meant to be 'gotchas' or may be super specific to a covered topic. For example, though the understanding of unix commands is useful in various industries, some questions often relied on a lab discussion slide that may have shown some [blank] unix command that was only used then and never really again - to some students those questions may have felt like free points, but when you are handling other courses and only have a vague idea of what was to be on a quiz then, some miscellaneous unix command would feel like the least of your concerns. I personally scored poorly on exams and quizzes, at some point getting a 39% on an exam, fully tanking my grade and putting my chances to pass on the line. However, because I submitted all other projects, exercises and labs on time, my grade was brought up just enough to pass. Yoon as a person: Results may vary, but Yoon was extremely approachable and a kind understanding person. If you come to his office hours or email him, he is very helpful and he often responds to emails fast. I often stayed after class to ask clarifications or questions related to the content, so did others, and he would make sure to explain and breakdown your confusions so as to help you better understand the material. Moreover, if you maintain a clear communication with him about certain issues and difficulties, he is often willing to help and possibly grant small extensions for submissions. He is also kind of funny when you talk to him one on one. I would say, do not walk into his class seeing him as some sort of evil supervillain who sits in his office petting a cat while plotting on how to make his next exam as hard as possible. If I am not mistaken, being a professor is not his main role at the university, take a look at his extensive resume and his achievements and you sort of realize that being a professor is just a role he has to play, and he can only have so much time on his hands, so balancing all of these responsibilities may be difficult even for him. p.s. I say all this not to glaze him or hype him up, all I'm saying is that it's probably a lot of work to handle but he's kinda goated if you talk to him - ppl like to make him seem like the devil himself but he's not too bad. Yoon as a professor: Now, regarding his teaching style, it is not really the best. Attend all his lectures and you will realize that not many people show up. He likes to go into a lot of specific niche topics while teaching, and he likes to draw on his iPad to breakdown what may be happening regarding memory allocation and a lot of other visual stuff. Often times this gets confusing, as he draws on top of the slides, so it is not visually the best and easiest thing to understand unless you follow along closely. If you walk into class during these moments when he draws out topics, it looks like that one picture 'my coworker losing his damn mind.' Moreover, sometimes he goes back and forth with a lot of coding examples and slides, so it gets a little confusing to understand. It is hard to keep up sometimes and it would often require revisiting his recorded lectures to get a better grasp of a concept. On the times he does do well, then it is easy to follow along and he does a good job at walking you through some of the topics. Class logistics: Honestly not the best at all. This semester, we only found out about our exam results a couple of days before the final. Some projects take a long time to get graded, and the late submission turns on after 11 pm of the on-time submission date. Some lab works were graded the day of grade submissions, so it was difficult to truly get what your grade was throughout the semester. Honestly, it was gut wrenching not knowing where you stood before the final exam was added to the grade book. He would often curve, at some point in some semester he curved by 8% percent, but this semester he did not curve as much, only slightly so that was also gut wrenching. TLDR: Not the worst. His exams and quizzes are hard and may tank your grade, but the class is set up in a way where your grade is able to take it if you do all the other work on time. As a person he's chill, as a professor he struggles, your grade will never be truly clear until the day grades are due. If you have to take him because every other section is closed prepare for a rough semester but if you show up and do your best you should be good. Not truly the end of the world, but you will have to put your head down to make it through.

All of the projects, homeworks, labs are very useful and relevant for the exams. Nothing in this class is that difficult, but make sure to start on all projects as soon as possible. Kauffman is always heavy on catching cheating on the projects; just give them your best honest attempt and I promise you'll be fine. His slides are very wordy and often contain a lot of useless information, and you're meant to rely more on your own work for the exams (they are open resource). Kauffman is by far the best choice for 216 right now and it's not really close.

This class wasn't too bad, but it really did not feel as rewarding as how the other reviews said. The structure of the class was split upon 5 projects, 2 exams and a final, hw "quizzes" (they're incredibly easy), and labs. The projects were quite difficult in my opinion (3 and 4 were the worst but in different ways I'll explain later), but they were good practice. PLEASE DO NOT GPT THEM, AND PLEASE START THEM EARLY (trust me you will thank me). They are designed in a way that is quite different from 132, they are very long and test the information covered very thoroughly, but it is good preparation for the exams. I thought projects 1, 2, and 5 were relatively straight forward with a decent difficulty, project 3 was pretty difficult because Assembly is just like that, but project 4 was really hard because it was assigned late (giving us less time to do it), but it was an added difficulty to figure out which tests were actually passing because it would work half of the time due to the concurrency (sometimes, tests would pass on grace, but completely fail on gradescope). The exams were actually not that bad, they tested content covered in the most recent project to the date of the exam, and were open note. But I think the open note concept is kind of misleading, it doesn't make the exam easier, it just made it a different kind of test, covering applications of topics rather than fundamentals which you may be used to from previous courses. The labs and hw were good practice if you diligently did them, and were overall pretty free grades. Kauffman did pretty unique system for participation called engagement points, which was counted under the labs you completed, but any extra lecture participation or piazza participation would be counted as bonus, which was pretty nice. The content covered wasn't really too difficult in my opinion, but I felt lectures were pretty pointless. His slides I felt made the content way more complicated than it needed to be, and his speaking was way too technical and not easily digestible. During discussion, a lot of times it felt the TAs couldn't really give me in depth answers for questions, and mainly just went over the labs. As a result, most of the time I would learn either reading the textbook or yt vids. A lot of times too, the way Kauffman focused on materials was more related to computer hardware than the coding, which made it feel more applicable for Computer Engineering majors, and I felt like the mateiral I was learning was kinda useless compared to how other teachers taught it. Some of the reviews talked about him being condescending, others talked about him being super nice, I thought he was fine overall in approachability, but like none of the extremes. TL;DR: Kauffman is a fine professor, but I felt a lot of the reviews are rating him higher than he should be. The course content isn't too difficult, but sometimes it was pretty frustrating using the terminal and grace, and its just overall a very different experience from previous courses. Once again DO NOT GPT PROJECTS, START THEM ON DAY 1 - you will suffer the consequences otherwise trust me.

Goes on rants during class and wastes valuable class time correcting students on their phones and trying to pick up from where he left off. He wastes like an average of 2 minutes per lecture doing this. Projects are horrendously long, he likes to throw stupid curveball questions on exams, won't teach certain chapters from the book and just assigns it as reading and then tests hard on it on exams like you're a jackass with a blindfold. Discussions are pointless besides picking up the worksheet, which most of the time can be done alone outside of discussion time. If you have a tight, hard schedule in your semester coming up, don't take this guy as he's like a greedy goblin taking up your valuable study time like it's gold.

I genuinely like Yoon as a professor and of the CS professors I've had at UMD so far, Yoon is my favorite. Though it's obvious I'm odd in that. He's really nice and will happily answer any questions you have, and its very clear he cares about teaching the class and students success. I don't think there's anything that feels unfair at any point, and he often puts up clarifications (or honestly borderline hints) for exams/quizzes part way through for common clarifications. I think the projects could have clearer explanations on the assignment docs, but I think some of that comes down to real-world edge cases or behavior vs what they want to and can test in a class project sometimes not lining up exactly. I will say, the TAs were very hit or miss this semester and any real complaints I do have about the course come down to that, since the grading can take a while and some of the responses on piazza were not the best.

THE GOAT! Very good lecturer, the quizzes are very similar to the homework and worksheets. Even his exams aren't very difficult if you do the problems from class and discussions. He does speak a bit fast, but it's a fun class, and he's super funny! Honestly, the reason why I got a bad grade was mostly on me.

Fawzi is a very good lecturer and breaks down topics very well. The quizzes and tests are honestly not very difficult, and there are never any surprises. Make sure you come to every class and pay attention during lecture, which is easy to do considering that Fawzi is an entertaining lecturer. Getting the grade you want shouldn't be too hard as long as you spend a bit of time reviewing for each test and go to discussion.
Amazing professor. Teaches well and is very passionate. Quizzes were easy, and the midterms + final were all fair.

Max is genuinely a very disorganized professor, his lectures at the beginning of the semester started off with lots of mistakes, errors, and he tends to go through material a lot quicker than necessary. Closer to the middle of semester he started getting better for lectures but it always felt like he came in unprepared for what he was going to teach and as if he didn't even review the slideshow he would be putting on the screen. Additionally there was a huge lack of timely completion for our homeworks and exams and it constantly felt like we were guessing what our grade in the class was. Piazza responses from the instructors were also so limited and it felt like the TAs also had no idea what was going on. Most of them barely responded to questions in Piazza. Grading: Pros: - very good weight distribution where 50% of the grade from discussion worksheets and homework - 3 exams and a final was nice because each exam was less weight (12%, 12%, 12%, 14%) - Extra credit (.05%) for things such as piazza posts, every 3 lecture notes and in class practices Cons: - exams were all over the place, with the first two exam averages being in the 70's range - first exam had a question that Max wasn't even able to fully solve in lecture - homework grading was much harsher than expected considering in lecture Max would often skip steps that the homeworks expected us to provide

Decent professor. Definitely cares about what he's teaching an explains concepts really well. He is slightly slower and doesn't cover as much content as other professors though. Grade consists of weekly quizzes, homework, 2 midterms, and a final. Quizzes are pretty hit or miss, but eventually you get the hang of it. Personally, I felt the quizzes were harder than the exams cuz they kind of test you on trickier stuff. Homeworks are definitely hard but are good preparation for the exam in terms of what to expect. Start on them early and ask TAs about any questions you have to maximize your grade for them. Exams are pretty easy. If you just study for them, you're guaranteed a 90+. They are straightforward questions and even if you can't figure out a proof, you can reason your way to something to get most of the credit. Grading can be a bit harsh for homework assignments and sometimes the quizzes as well, but just submit a regrade or ask Teli and he can fix it. The final wasn't actually a final. It was just a third midterm. Only covered whatever we learned after the 2nd midterm. Was also about the same length as the other midterms, maybe slightly longer, but you get twice the amount of time. Overall, I thought it was a well ran class. Because it is a discrete math course, some of the things might not click immediately. But he records lectures so you can look back at them and if you go to his office hours, he is very open to answering any questions or doubts you have. I would definitely say Teli is a good option for 250

Pretty good professor! Lectures aren't super engaging but they get the job done. Concepts are explained pretty clearly and while I did leave some lectures confused, I found rewatching the recordings always helped me understand the material. All lectures were recorded and his handwritten notes showed up in the recording and they were also posted as pdfs to canvas. Homeworks were a bit difficult but were VERY helpful in preparing students for the exams. He gives weekly quizzes in discussions, always on Wednesday's, and these were alright. The pressure of a weekly quiz does force you to stay on top of the material which sounds bad but I felt it made it so that I barley had to study for the midterms since I had put effort into learning the material for the quizzes. The exams themselves are not overly difficult, they do reflect what's taught and have very fair grading. He also rounded up grades if you were 1% away from a different letter grade, and while that didn't benefit me, it was still very generous of him to do. In general, Teli is a very good professor and worth taking!

Alright professor. Teaches well and goes in depth on topics. Exams and really easy, much easier than the quizzes and homework assignments. Pretty similar to the practice exams he provides in terms of difficulty. The only downside are the quizzes. They are pretty hit or miss, and you have study a good amount to do well on it. They are a pretty small part of your grade though so it isn't too bad. Homeworks aren't bad but can take time to complete, so start early. The questions are pretty difficult and the format of answers can be a bit abstract. Definitely to office hours to make sure your doing things correctly. Overall, the class isn't too bad. Lectures can be a bit boring or confusing, but just watch the lecture videos and you should be fine. He's a really nice person and does a good job in explaining concepts. Would definitely recommend for 250l

Fawzi might be the best professor I have ever had honestly. Coming into the class, the content seemed to be pretty intimidating, but he taught in a way that made everything stick completely. The grading was broken down simply by just quizzes and exams. I felt the exams were really easy, legit just testing if you understood the fundamentals of the topics he taught, with no real curveballs. Final was a bit harder, but that is to be expected. I will say though, lectures were pretty boring, because he would basically spend the entire time doing a bunch of practice problems, but I found this to actually be super helpful as I just learnt a lot more this way personally. Though, this made discussion virtually pointless, there would basically just be a worksheet, and thats about it. As for the quizzes, fawzi's policy would be that there may or may not be a quiz during discussion, as an effort to motivate people to go. But, this just basically turned into a lot of people coming just during the last 20 min of class, because quizzes would always occur during that time. As for Fawzi himself, some have said that he says some weird things during class (which he kinda does), but this didn't really affect me too much. Other than that, the way he taught just really stuck with me, and I felt like he really set us up well for the exams (averages were pretty high too). Definitely would recommend for this class.

It's funny how the reviews for Fawzi are so divided! Let me make this easy for you: If you want to learn the CMSC250 material, then you 100% want to take Fawzi's class! He is the best lecturer (best teacher) I've ever seen. He's engaging, inspiring, and at times hilarious! If you're lazy or just not a good student and you want to pass CMSC250 without doing any work, without coming to class, and without learning anything, then he is not your guy. People are whining because Fawzi expects them to actually put in some effort! Maybe switch your major. Upper level classes are gonna be a lot worse. It's not really that hard to get an A in this class. You need to come to all the lectures, study the things he says are important, finish all the worksheets that are given out in the discussions, and keep up with the homework questions (which are not graded). The questions on quizzes and exams are totally reasonable and the grading is fair. The exam medians were always over 80%. He also gave quizzes that were super easy and totally predictable (if you've been keeping up). At the end of the semester he posted an announcement with the final grade distribution: 31% got an A+/A/A- 34% got a B+/B/B- 25% got a C+/C/C- 10% were D+/D/F What's the problem? This was my favorite class so far at UMD. I went in knowing nothing about how to write a proof and honestly, now I kind of feel like an expert at it!

I don't understand why so many people dislike Fawzi. Personally, I think that he's an amazing lecturer who sets up all of his students for success. He explains everything very thoroughly, and has great lecture slides/examples. His lectures can get boring sometimes, but they're usually fine. His quizzes are very similar in difficulty, if not slightly easier, than the homework questions. Some of his exam questions are sometimes confusing, but they are also very similar in difficulty to homework questions and very doable. All of his exams and quizzes are usually graded within a few days, and the TAs provide pretty thorough feedback on Gradescope. The only reason that I ended with an A- instead of an A or A+ is because I stopped going to classes (got lazy lmao) after Exam #1. Though I was able to still do fine by studying the lecture slides, doing the homework, and asking ChatGPT for help, I recommend just going to lectures to keep yourself sane. Also, even though he said there were many students doing amazing in his class, my 88.3 still became an A-

Fall 2025 Pros: - Very funny and engaging guy. Seems like a cool guy to just hang out with - Explanations are very clear - Expectations for proof formats are very clear. He makes it very clear what you will need to do to get all the points on proofs - Homeworks are not graded, and keys are posted, giving you more flexibility - If you do all the homeworks and discussion worksheets, you will be fine. No outside practice needed. Cons: - Grading is potentially challenging: 80% exams, 20% quizzes - Weekly scheduled/announced (not pop) quizzes in discussion, but all of them are fair and reflect what is on the homework (either the most recent one, or the second to most recent one) Other: - Lectures are not recorded, as Fawzi expects everyone to come to class. But of all the lecturers I’ve had at UMD, Fawzi has the most valuable lectures, so I wouldn’t skip. Grade Statistics Exam 1: Median: 88% Exam 2: Median: 87% Exam 3: Median: 78% Final: Median: 76% Median course grade: 82% (~35% of people get A’s) Overall definitely take Fawzi. He teaches very well, makes it easy to do well, and is a chill/funny guy.

teli is a pretty decent professor. 3 exams (2 midterms and 1 final). lecturing is alright, but overall lectures are pretty boring and hard to get through. quizzes are free grades and homeworks + exams are graded pretty strictly by TA's. shoutout to Shashaank who was genuinely the best TA ive ever had and probably will ever have. discussion is really what made this class for me and without it I probably would have done much worse with just Teli's lecturing. overall he's okay but you are probably better off with Fawzi.

this class was SO easy because of how practical its content is. i literally got a 101% and probably spent at most a 5 hours a week outside of the class on it. you learn about functional programming and regex which are extremely important concepts no matter what language youre using, and then you get to learn a little about compilers which is fun! cliff at times would start talking about something without saying what we were learning about which was a bit confusing, but everyone got the point eventually. he did put me to sleep a bit, but you can understand him still! i am someone who prefers a more organized teaching style usually, but i enjoyed his more laid back approach for a change tbh. this class is also the complete opposite of 351, if you do the practice exams you will know every single question type that is on the exam/quizzes and there are no curveballs. he was a little slow on releasing the projects, but he made them easier when he was behind so im not complaining.

Doesn't really matter if you take Cliff or Anwar for 330 as they coteach (same quizzes, projects, exams, schdule, etc.). If you don't like Cliff's slides/notes, you can see Anwars. If you don't like Cliff's lectures, you can go to Anwar's. I went to both and Cliff is slightly more boring, but I also find the material boring in general. This is easily one of the most organized CS classes at UMD. Everything is very clearly communicated, and the course website is SO useful. Every past quiz and exam is available to study with (as well as their answer keys). The TAs are obviously very close with Cliff and seem to enjoy their job, which definitely makes the class feel sort of homey. My biggest complaint were some exam questions were really more reliant on logic than on understanding of the material. These were the minority, but still. Also, Cliff/Anwar both sent out an email to everyone, accusing "many" people in the class of using AI on projects. They offered the usual "snitch on yourself or we'll sent you to OSC". My problem with this is that they sent this a few weeks before finals, and said they wouldn't report anyone until after finals. So, everyone had the threat of a potential false AI accusation looming over their heads throughout finals. Imo it's unprofessional to punish the entire class instead of just contacting/reporting those individuals directly. Also I knew of no one who was reported, so it's somewhat likely they lied to get people to turn themselves in (since you can't really prove AI usage anyways).

This class was extremely boring, not well taught, and not useful. Projects were fine. He seems nice as a professor but I could not for the life of me stay focused while he rambles on just pointing at stuff in the code editor in a dry voice. I wouldn't recommend taking this course.

He is genuinely a very kind and approachable professor, and I would definitely recommend taking CMSC330 with him. While the course itself is certainly challenging, he creates a welcoming and supportive learning environment that truly makes a difference. He is patient, warm, and always considerate of his students, which helps make a difficult class feel much more manageable and encouraging.

I didn’t have the best experience with Dr. Mamat in CMSC330. While he clearly knows the material well, I found his lectures to be hard to follow and often disorganized. He would jump between code and slides without clearly connecting the concepts, which made it difficult to take notes or understand how everything fit together. It felt like he was more focused on getting through the material than making sure students were following along. He didn’t use lecture recordings, which made it hard to review anything after class, especially for students who learn better by rewatching. I also thought the code demos, while sometimes helpful, were rushed and lacked thorough explanation. The projects were challenging, and while they were aligned with course content, there wasn’t much support beyond office hours, which filled up fast. There were also times where project specs felt vague or assumed knowledge we hadn’t really covered in lecture yet. The exams were fair, but since most of the emphasis was on the projects, you didn’t get much feedback on how well you were doing until it was too late to make major improvements. Overall, I think the class could benefit from clearer organization, better pacing in lectures, and more structured support for the assignments. I wouldn’t recommend taking CMSC330 with Dr. Mamat unless you’re really confident learning independently.

The class honestly isn't too difficult content wise. Ocaml coding can be a slightly annoying (especially with lexer, parser, interpreter) and the rust rules may be difficult to understand at first but in the grand scheme of the course, nothing is that hard to pick up. I know many people say Anwar is an entertaining lecturer, but I went to almost every class and struggled to pay attention in every single one. Luckily he started giving out attendance extra credit but as far as what he actually taught, it was awfully boring for me and I found myself doing homework for other classes. I basically taught myself all of the content while studying for the exam or doing the projects. You could honestly chose either Cliff or Anwar for this course but it seems like Anwar was more lenient, especially with his extra credit.

Anwar is nice, but I really hate his style of teaching since it’s so disengaging. Literally on the first day of class, bro just opened up Emacs and started coding random stuff, and he doesn’t reference his slides at all—or barely does. It’s so hard to take notes or follow along with what he’s saying when he just codes for 1 hour and 15 minutes straight. Towards the end of February, I started skipping lecture since he unnecessarily went way too in-depth into the concepts and didn’t teach them in the way they actually show up on the quizzes and exams. This class is nice though because the projects make up a big portion of your grade, and the exams aren’t worth that much. I literally failed the first two exams and did well on the final, and I’m ending with an A- (that’s telling you how easy it is to do well in this class if you lock in on projects). Quizzes are each only 2% of your grade, but they add up so try to do well on them. You just need to study 2–3 days in advance and do the practice, and you’ll be fine. The TAs are way more involved in this course compared to other CS classes, but some of them clearly don’t know anything about the concepts. Also, some are super unhelpful on Piazza, not directly answering questions but posting a link to something else because they're too lazy to answer LMAO. But luckily my discussion TA was super smart, so he was very helpful. Overall, this class won’t be too bad, but try to study in advance and get ahead if you can to make it easier on yourself.

Dr. Mamat is a great professor. He genuinely cares about his students and wants them to succeed, and he wants to cultivate a classroom environment where students feel comfortable and willing to ask questions. He is very good at tying complex concepts into real-life examples to make them more understandable. His exams were fair, and preparation for them helped me to understand the concepts better. However, I am not the biggest fan of Dr. Mamat's lecture style, I wish he would follow notes/slides a bit more strictly, as sometimes the concepts are a little difficult to follow when taking notes due to lack of clear structure. I also wish he would relate his lectures to the projects a little more, or at least be clearer with which aspects of the content would be on the projects. Many times it felt like I was learning the most from the projects, which isn't necessarily a bad thing, but still I wish that I was a little more prepared going into the projects. Still, he records all his lectures so if you ever feel lost you can go back and watch the recording. His class was tough but if you put in the work you will be fine. My breakdown of his exams/projects: Exam 1: hardest exam, average was a 67 Exam 2: harder content, but written to be easier than exam 1, average was a 78 Projects 1-2 weren't too bad. Projects 3 and 4 start to get more difficult. Project 5 was definitely the hardest. Project 6 was a breeze (intro to Rust). Project 7 TBD.

This class is probably the worst out of all the lower level CS classes in its current state being ran by Mamat and Cliff. It is ran very unprofessionally and poorly. Dr. Mamat, despite being a funny guy, was a horrible lecturer. He would complain that less people came to class but his lectures were genuinely so hard to follow. He posts lecture videos though which you can rewatch and there are Cliff's notes, so you can still do a decent amount of learning on your own. The previous exam solutions had a lot of errors which never got fixed, and you needed to check on piazza to see if someone had clarified the solutions were wrong for a specific question, which was annoying. It was also annoying when we randomly started doing rust with 2 weeks left to go in the semester, and the specifications for the final exam were "anything mentioned in any lecture/discussion/quiz/project up until the last day of class." Overall it's definitely not impossible to do well in this class, it's just ran very poorly which makes it frustrating at times.

To be honest, I did NOT like Cliff as a professor whatsoever. First lecture was good, then it went downhill from there. All Cliff does is yap his mind away and it is very hard to pay attention in lectures because he has a very monotone voice and very boring slides. At some point, I stopped going to lecture, and even if I did, I would NOT pay attention. By the middle/end of the semester, there were only 50 kids in the lecture hall. His slides are alright, but tbh, Anwar's material helped me learn the material better. Looking back, the concepts in 330 are not bad whatsoever, just that Ocaml is so annoying to write. He has quizzes that were aight (first one was atrocious, avg was a 9/20). His exams were also pretty straightword, not many curveballs other than putting brainrot on questions that made the question so much harder to understand and solve. PROJECTS 3-5 ARE BAD, START EARLY. For these projects, he made them longer/harder because of cheating in previous semesters. Tbh, I might recommend anwar over cliff but it really doesnt matter.

Good teacher overall. He's a boring lecturer imo but you can easily just not go to class because his pdf notes are very well-constructed and cover everything you need. OCaml is tough, and projects 3-5 are the hardest I've had in college so far. Thankfully, only 10-20% of each exam is OCaml coding because 330 is really just a "intro to compilers" course and the theoretical/non-coding topics are pretty easy. Rust is fine. Choosing between him and Anwar doesn't matter. They share exams, projects, quizzes, and the course website, so just pick the better time.

sometimes i called him clifford baka Pros: Class overall is not as bad as the horror stories suggest Projects were interesting He's funny Exams were fair Cons: It was kinda funny how no one paid attention in lecture, he's not terrible but he's very monotone and it does get hard to pay attention Some of the projects were BRUTAL but looking back kinda straightforward, def go to office hours if you can snag a TA they are helpful Overall, you'll be fine, go to office hours

As the last review says, this is also my first time writing a review (that says enough already). This is by far the most disorganized, poorly structured, and poorly taught class I have ever taken by far. If you've walked by Kruskal's office in Iribe, that about says enough. Now for some perhaps, a structure where you learn from poorly recorded videos and irrelevant in-person lectures is just a blast, where you don't know midterm and final topics until 5 days before the exams. Comparing this to taking CMSC330 simultaneously, this is a complete 180, where that class has plenty of practice material, notes and lecture recordings available, etc. You have nothing to study for on exams, expect practically needing to memorize all the relevant recorded lectures, as well as having a chance at a problem or two appearing from a previous exam a few years back. Note that all you are provided for study material, is a previous exam, and irrelevant practice questions that exist to get you to think critically, and that's about it. Even then, 1/3 of the questions you will be faced, are "thinking" problems that have little relevance to anything you've learned. Quizzes are due the morning of the in person lecture, and often aren't announced or posted until the evening of the day before, so god forbid you're studying or doing work for another class, you have no choice but to focus on that first. In terms of the TAs, it's almost as if they tried to find the most fitting ones, in correspondence to what the professor is like. The TAs (like the professor himself) never reply to emails, or if you get lucky, one will reply in a week, asking questions in Piazza is also useless, I really don't even know why this class had one. They took forever to grade things, even simple multiple choice quizzes, not to mention not getting your exam feedback returned until the next exam comes around. As for the professor, he is more likely than not on tenure, concerns have been voiced regarding his teaching millions of times, and nothing has been done. You will not even get a GLANCE at what your grade is in the class, til grades are due (note that your final and other quizzes are still not graded, that's why I said a glance). You will spend your whole semester biting your nails and ripping your hair out, not knowing what the cutoff will be like, how you're doing in the class, and what curveball will be thrown at you next. If that isn't enough, grades were due on Dec. 23, and get ready for this...ITS THE NEXT YEAR and there is still no grade in Testudo. It's important to add, that Kruskal grades on a curve, so when you see the class averaging 50 on exams, I wouldn't think too much of it, as I'll provide the cutoffs below. I don't understand how this could be the best way to go about grading for this class. Why not create manageable problems, curriculum, exams, etc. and grade on a more normal scale, perhaps adjusting cutoffs, like all the other CS classes do? Note that as far as I understand it, as long as you land within the standard deviation on exams, given your quiz and homework grades are good enough, you will pass. I certainly have a lot more on my mind that I've gathered throughout the semester, but for the reader's purposes I will end it off on some tips and class information. Tips: -Memorize those lecture recordings like there's no tomorrow. This is practically a self-learning course, and this is about 99% of your learning resources. -Do the Homeworks yourself! Only once finished check with your friend or ChatGPT. Exams will consist of one or two reworded homework problems. -In-person lectures are mostly a waste of time, unless you're genuinely curious about the irrelevant information provided to you, so use that time to study instead. -Your best practice material will be a previous year's exam and your homeworks, and those consist of 50 to 66% of the exam -For the "thinking" problems, try to get some practice online or from Leetcode, based on what the "thinking" problem is like from the previous exam -Only rely on all of the above if you were not able to get a spot in Justin's class, but I would do everything in my ability to go back and take his class instead. Before I provide grading metrics, I want to summarize that this is a counter-intuitive, self-learning course, with an elderly hard of hearing professor, who doesn't plan on changing or modifying the structure of his course whatsoever. I am by any means not a morning person, but I do consider myself to be organized, so if sacrificing those early hours of sleep and not losing my ability to sleep is available (along with receiving a better education), choose Justin, and do everything in your power to do so. Last but not least, even with the tips provided above, you will still often encounter problems on exams that are nothing like what you studied for, and it's not uncommon to encounter a topic which lacks fundamental resources to even know what to expect from it. Either start meditating or choose a faith, because that's all that can protect your mental state here. https://www.cs.umd.edu/classconcern Cutoffs for THIS semester: -A+: 85.0 - 100, A: 75.0 - 84.99, A-: 70.0 - 74.99, B+: 65.0 - 69.99, B: 60.0 - 64.99, B-: 55.0 - 59.9, C+: 51.0 - 54.99, C: 46.0 - 50.99, C-: 41.0 - 45.99 Exam Statistics THIS semester: -Midterm 1: Avg: 56.6, Std Dev: 22 -Midterm 2: Avg: 58.5, Std Dev: 25.94 -Final: Avg: 56.61, Std Dev: 16.53 Grading Percentages THIS semester: 25% — Midterm 1, 25% — Midterm 2, 33% — Final exam, 7% — Regular Homeworks, 4% — NP Homeworks, 6% —Quizzes -6 lowest quizzes were dropped Good luck.

Obviously avoid this man like the plague if you can. BUT, if you're stuck taking him, understand that you will not learn anything whatsoever. There is literally zero point in expecting to actually get better at algorithms after taking 351 with Kruskal. Lectures are completely laughable. Better off not attending them tbh. TAs are... there. I mean, go say hi, little chit-chat. Not much actual help though. You learn quickly that this class is one of the more unorthodox "sink-or-swim" experiments, if we can call it that. Btw, don't expect any semblance punctuality with grading whatsoever. Also the class is very exam-heavy. And that too only 3 exams that make up 83% of your grade. That's very stressful. And the Homeworks aren't graded that leniently either. No place for free points. And make sure you do as well as you can on the midterms because the final is just brutal. We got back our second midterm grades a week before the final. Also half the grades (as far back as grades before the first midterm took place) wasn't even graded one week before the exam. No point stressing, just chill. Best way to study for the exams is to a) Understand all the past exam and practice exam material, and the homework problems and try think of different spins on them, different variations they can be asked in, etc. This helps SO much. b) Understand the algorithms and all topics covered on the exams extremely well conceptually. c) Memorize, and then change up and iterate whatever practice exam material questions provided. If you do these things, the exams are quite frankly not extremely hard. Just not enough time to answer everything with your best attempt. Honestly, other than the final I kinda winged the exams, maybe spent a day or so studying for them. What I did not do was care to reference any of the lectures or try and learn things on the go. There are maybe 1 or 2 questions that require you to use your intuition and reason quickly. But otherwise, the exams are just practice exam and homework questions reworded or asked with some kind of twist. The optimal approach is to answer those quickly first, and then spend a lot of time to reason out the 1 or 2 thinking-heavy questions The exams are graded extremely harshly. Again, you'll get so many points off even when you are conceptually correct because of some little thing. You HAVE to push regrade requests after every exam. Otherwise you leave a ton of points on the table. You'll still probably do terrible on the exams. But so will everyone else. Our exam averages were 57, 58, and 43. So make of that what you will. The one saving grace of this class is the all ole mighty curve. For this Semester: A+: 85+, A: 75+, A-: 70+, B+: 65+, B: 60+, B-: 55+, C+: 51+, C: 46+, C-: 41+. I **thought** I did much better on the final than I actually ended up scoring. Grading, like I mentioned before, ridiculous how ridiculous it is. Just farm regrade requests if you answered a question mostly correctly but got all or most points off. One thing though was that I ended up being surprised by the relative generosity of the curve, so there's that. So for that reason and that reason alone, I'm giving this 2 stars rather than 1.

I found his personality very interesting but dear god where his exams the worst exams I have ever taken. 85% was dictated on 1-2 midterms and a final which aimed to ask a handful of complex problems instead of a lot of smaller ones. I took him for 351 which is all about algorithms and efficiency but this meant that if you just didn't have a good idea for an algorithm to tackle the question he asks then that is 20 points off the exam. There is a shaky relationship with partial credit since the TAs are grading off of his pretty strict rubric that demands a baseline level of efficiency otherwise you receive no credit. This practice doesn't pair well with his very unique classroom strategy of "class is optional 90% of the time but you have to watch a lecture I recorded years ago and answer a few questions about it to learn the material and we will discuss that in class." TLDR: If you are not a very outgoing/proactive student (similar to me) this class will most likely be tough. P.S. the mean for all three exams we took was ~55 with a standard deviation of ~20. He curved it based on standard deviation from the mean which means a 41 passes with a C-.

This class is easy if you rather learn it yourself, otherwise avoid it like the plague. The tests are actually pretty easy because the questions are very similar to the hw he gives. However, most people don’t learn anything due to lectures being in the form of videos on panapto, causing very low test scores(averages and medians of about 50% for every exam) even though the tests are often easier than hw. If you can learn the content, the class is pretty easy, if you need an actual professor good luck.

Professor Kruskal is a good lecturer, a nice man to talk to, and very knowledgeable on the topic of algorithms. However, in terms of class structure, grading, and anything administrative, the class is an absolute disaster. The exam material is not taught in class, rather in prerecorded online lectures (so if you want to go to his in-person classes you effectively double your time spent in class), currently only 19/52 assignments in the course have been graded with under 2 weeks until the final, so it is extremely difficult to get a gauge on how you are doing in the course (which isn't helped by the fact that the averages for the exams have been a 52 and a 63, so there is also a curve you need to take into account), and Prof. Kruskal himself seems unwilling to ever change these things about the course, for reasons he will share with his students if they are so inclined. Overall, stay ahead of the curve, use past homework to study for the exams, and you will do okay in the course, but it is not a fun experience. I truly think Kruskal could have a much higher reputation if he just began covering exam content in person, but it doesn't seem like that will change any time soon.

I think this is the last time Justin is teaching 351 in-semester for a while, but certainly take him when you can. I'll be taking him for 420. We shall see how the 7-exam system works, but 351 is as of writing the more traditional 3 50-minute exams and a final which are 83.33% (500 of 600 points) of the course. Pros: - He's done this so many times that he can guide everyone from start to end of the topics and the course without many hiccups. - The entire official class notes are already posted, and he just rewrites a section of them for each lecture. - Exams are straighforward, follow the 3ish practice exams you get. They're always the same difficulty level, no curveballs. - You'll lose a few points on the homeworks here and there but they're generally helpful for understanding stuff. - Great guy living a great life. If you need anything, he'll help where he can. - Can't emphasize enough how nice it is to not have shenanigans going on in a class. No games to be played, just learn content however you do best, do homeworks, take exams, repeat. Cons? - Really the only downside that I could imagine is that he keeps having to simplify the course, so we go slower than we could and don't learn as much as we used to. It's cool stuff, and I wouldn't mind learning more of it. - I guess I should mention 351 has no discussion (didn't see a need for one), and Justin doesn't do recordings, which again there's daily handwritten notes and the permanent full body of LaTeX notes. For me, it was worth getting up at 6am to commute and go to all the 8am lectures. Prioritize getting a seat in a Justin section.

This was overall a pretty great class and I would highly recommend Justin. The break down was: 1/6 - 10 Homework Assignments (1 a week apart from exam weeks) 3/6 - 3 Midterm Exams 2/6 - 1 Final Exam The homework's often had a few problems which were more difficult than what you would see on an exam, but doing them really helped you learn the material and made sure you really understood what was being taught. The exams themselves were very fair. There was never any problem which was a complete curve ball, and if you understood the material, they were very doable. He also posts very detailed hand written lecture notes, so even if you miss class, you can easily just look at the notes. I overall found this class to pretty interesting and I thought Justin did a great job explaining the concepts during lecture.

I know its said quite often but Justin really is a great lecturer. Even at 8 am, he did a great job at explaining concepts and his notes are really great. The exams were a bit difficult, not because of the content being difficult but just because small mistakes have a lot of weight for your exam and its hard to get partial credit on objective solutions. Even so, his notes were concise enough that you could study for an exam fairly easily and he also gave many hints in the class before the exam as to what was on it and what to prepare for. He also truly cares about every student in the class and even went as far to give a curve for the final grade cutoffs which really helped. He took note of how long people were taking on the final and put that in consideration which was also great. Even though a large portion of the final grades were exams (50% exams, 33% final), its definitely possible to come out with a good grade in the class with not too much effort.

Do not take Kruskal unless you 100% for certain have to. If you have to, heres what to expect - Cons: You will not get a syllabus until two weeks in. You will not know what date your midterms are until a week before. You not be given study material until a few days before an exam. The study material you do get will not be like the exam at all (do previous semesters exams instead, those are good). Your assignments will not be graded in time for midterms and the final, so you wont know if you are doing well or not. You will spend more time trying to figure out what the homework is asking you to do than actually doing it. The exams consist of reworded homework questions + "thinking" problems that you have not seen before, and it is up to your intellect and intuition to solve it. You learn via pre-made lecture videos, some are good, some are bad. In-person lectures are basically just discussions where you go over quizzes and he talks about irrelevant topics that wont be on the exam. Pros: You will do bad in this class, but so will everyone else. The curve is generous. This semester, the cutoffs were A+: 86+ A: 77-86 A-: 71.5-77 B+: 67.5-71.5 B: 63-67.5 B-: 59-63 C+: 55.5-59 C: 52-55.5 C-: 48.5-52 D: 35-48.5. Statistically you'll probably be fine in the end, but you wont enjoy doing it.

As other students have stated here, Ting is a nice person and seems approachable, and she clearly cares about her students' success. She’s somewhat active on Piazza and provides helpful advice on how to study and succeed in the class (e.g., prioritizing topics for midterms and the final). Unfortunately, the language barrier makes it difficult for her to communicate the material in a way that resonates with everyone. If you're comfortable understanding it, then more power to you. But for more security in your grade, I’d suggest the following approach: Attend Ting's first 2–3 lectures to gauge how well you can follow along. Then, consider attending Justin's lectures (I started around the Big-O/Big-Omega/Big-Theta topics) and see how you find them. Personally, I found Justin's co-teaching more helpful for understanding the material, even though it meant waking up at 8 AM. By the middle of the semester, I stopped attending Justin's lectures in person, instead reviewing his notes when doing the homework. I also used office hours to clarify concepts and help others out, which helped me practice the material. Occasionally, I’d attend Justin’s 8 AMs just to refresh my understanding. In hindsight, I probably should’ve been more consistent with attending Ting’s lectures, as my exam grades weren’t as high as they could’ve been. Grade Breakdown: MCQs: 7.69% Homework: 15.38% Exams: 46.15% Final Exam: 30.78% Total: 100% Most of the MCQs were straightforward, with about 14 in total this semester. You should be able to score 100% on most of them, though the number of MCQs and homework assignments may change each semester, as well as the number that are dropped. Many students, including myself, used a group chat to compare answers, and we attended office hours for clarification on more open-ended questions. While I didn’t go to Ting’s office hours, I’ve heard they’re helpful. The TAs for both Ting and Justin were shared this semester, and there were a few associated with Ting’s sections that I found especially helpful toward the end of the semester. As for Ting's exams, there’s a good amount of partial credit, so even if you make a few mistakes, it’s rare that you’d need to request a regrade. However, exams accounted for roughly 77% of our grade, so even if you perform well on homework and MCQs, you still need to do well on the midterms and final to get an A. If you handle exam pressure well and avoid small mistakes adding up, you should be in a strong position for this class.

Ting is a really nice and understanding professor. I had friends who took Justin and he didn't let them submit their homework even a little bit late or retake any quizzes; this one time I forgot about a lecture quiz and I emailed Ting and she was so kind and understanding; she let me redo it without any real explanation. Also she's super young so she's chill like I have literally seen her at the dining hall with her friends. I will say her lectures were really boring and hard to understand at times because of her accent so if you want to learn stuff with organized notes, go to Justin's lecture. I also 100% recommend taking Ting over Justin despite the reviews because, I cannot stress this enough: her exams were SO MUCH EASIER than Justin's. Justin exams always threw some kinda curveballs at you where you really have to understand the core of the concept or some crazy detail in the psuedocode while Ting's questions were mostly straightforward. The cheat code for this class is to source the exams from the previous semester because she reuses a lot of questions so keep that in mind it really helped me practice for the real thing, even though she did supply her own practice exams.

The worst professor i've ever had. This is the only time I have left a review and it's to tell you not to take her, her exams are unreasonably hard and barely reflect the material for 30% of the questions. The average on the exams is a C and she does not curve. If you miss class her notes are useless, and the homework's are grades very harshly. I am getting an B+ but this is still the worst teacher ever. The basics of this class shouldn't even be that hard looking back but she teaches and tests in such a way that it is nearly impossible to get a good grade.

I had him for fall 2025 Lectures are pretty boring, he mostly just reads from the textbook and stumbles over his words frequently, so its a good idea to have the textbook open while attending. He didn't collect homework this semester, but had frequent quizzes which were pretty much rehashed homework questions. As long as you do the homework, you'll be fine for the quizzes. Midterm exams were fair, but he threw a curveball for the final making it much harder than it probably should have. People below mentioned what he did so I'm not gonna repeat it here. At least he gave us a nice curve to compensate for it. tldr: If you have no other options, he's fine, but I wouldn't be jumping at the opportunity to take him again.

A bit of context, this is now a post-semester review being written roughly a week after final grades locked in. I already left a mid-semester review, where I gave Zheng only 4 stars to balance out the immediate 1-star review that someone left only 3 weeks into the semester. Now giving my afterthoughts: As the more recent reviews have already said, Zheng definitely improved over the semester, so I won’t restate everything they covered. I mainly want to give some context for why the early reviews were so harsh. After Midterm 1 (with an average of ~76), the GroupMe turned into an echo chamber where a vocal group of students circulated the same complaints for weeks. One of those students made a Google doc listing the issues with Zheng, and people in the chat were explicitly encouraging others to leave 1-star PlanetTerp reviews based on these complaints. That doc later got cleaned up into a printed letter addressed directly to Zheng, asking whether he could provide extra credit, more practice problems, drop the lowest midterms, etc. This letter was then left at the back of class for students to sign if they wanted to. To be fair, I’m not sure Zheng would have become as lenient as he did if that letter hadn’t happened, so some of that pushback was probably warranted, even if it shouldn’t take something like that for changes to happen in a class. Personally, I thought the Midterm 1 practice was definitely enough. I skipped a few problems that clearly weren’t exam-relevant, but most of them lined up with lecture examples or homework. Some questions were basically the same concept with different numbers. I get why people were frustrated, but a lot of the “nothing like the practice” claims were overstated. For Midterm 2 and the final, the practice was even closer to the exams; arguably too close in some cases. Zheng's sections were originally meant to be taught by Anurag Dey, who is known for being an "easy A" according to his PlanetTerp reviews for STAT401. We got switched to Zheng about a month before the fall semester started (i.e., during summer break), so it's possible that the same people were expecting that kind of class and were frustrated when the difficulty didn't match those expectations. I agree with another recent review that 3.5/5 is probably a more accurate rating, but I’m giving a 4 just to round up and reflect the improvement and to help improve the average. Zheng has shown he has the capacity to improve, communicates fairly well given the language barrier, and is certainly not the "worst" choice for STAT400 as other reviewers had initially put it.

He's a fine, default professor I'd say. His teaching is really straightforward and simple. He doesn't go too easy or too hard and his exams are really fair (EXCEPT THE FINAL) and the quizzes are trivial when you have a TA that basically gives you the answers beforehand. He curved everyone's grades insanely hard after everyone bombed the final he told us not to worry about so I really appreciate him for that because I KNOW I didn't earn this A, lol. People say he's boring but I don't think so. He teaches what's in the book and his exams don't throw curveballs. If he says "you'll have to do something like this on the exam" then chances are that exact question is word-for-word on the exam. All of this is an exception from the final literally do not trust what he says about the final because he straight up LIED to us about it. And then curved my B- into an A somehow, so.. I don't mind him too much. Soft recommend.

As said by other people, this class started out really rough. It was a pain and honestly I thought about dropping it more than a few times. However, after a letter and some feedback from the class, he did improve quite a bit. Definitely his first time teaching this course and it showed. He took lots of feedback and added curves, bonus points, and made the final pretty fair. In the future he has also promised to hand out bonus points in class for questions and such so make sure to participate. He has a bit of a thick accent so I would look at the textbook/youtube a bit. Pretty hard class, but mainly because it was his find time teaching it.

Hatice is very nice, I like her. But it's always very difficult to stay engaged during lecture, in part due to her style. This definitely isn't the hardest course in the world, and has a decent enough curve, but the exams were definitely some of the harder things I've taken.

In the second half of the semester, he got so much better. His midterm 2 was really good and he gave us a practice exam that was very similar to the actual exam. He also told us exactly what kind of problems would be on the exam. His final exam was extremely easy, it was almost a copy and paste of the practice exam he gave us before the final exam. It was his first time teaching so the first half of the semester was rough, but he listened to the students advice and really turned things around. The bad reviews were mostly all from the first half of the semester after the first midterm which went horribly.

I was a little bit harsh in my last review. but you have to go to lecture, simply looking over her posted notes is not enough because they are often missing important things she covered during lecture/on exams. She's nice though, just not organized on Canvas.

I'd say i'm the average-slightly below student. I got below the means on all exams. Exam 1 mean was 73, i got a 68, Exam 2 mean was 74, i got a 73, Final exam mean was a 133/200 (66), i got a 118/200 (59). Canvas said i got a 67.99% in the class, but i ended with a B-. He curved the hell out of the grades. Exam 1 and 2 were really fair content wise, if you did the quizzes, did the HW, it wouldve been really easy for you, i just freeze when it comes to exams. However, the final exam, was crazy, didnt really put much of the earlier topics you'd expect on there, and heavily focused on later end material with 2/6 questions being insane . It was 100% more difficult in my opinion then previous final exams. BUT, i would recommend him for this class. His quizzes are word for word based off the hw questions, just different numbers and slightly different wording (sometimes its word for word the same). If you do the homeworks which is just questions from the textbook, you will easily get a 100% on these, i averaged like a 89% on them. He teaches pretty well in the beginning, he sometimes get confusing as time goes on, especially if the topic is slightly more difficult because he just goes off textbook definitions. He does welcome questions, he stops the lecture several times to make sure no ones confused obviously were all too afraid to ask. You can 100% self teach yourself on this course though if you wanted. Watch youtube videos, look at the textbook (YT is better for the more complex ones), do the HW's, quizzes, attend OH if you cant find good enough YT vids for a HW, join the groupME for the class for any specific things he couldve said in the lecture about upcoming quizzes/exams, you'll be fine, trust.

The lectures were pretty unhelpful, I went to them all and rarely began to understand what he was saying until reading the textbook and doing practice problems. He also says "um" multiple times in every sentence which makes following along challenging. His exams are much more challenging than the homework's so it is really hard to practice doing problems with exam level difficulty. His syllabus says that you will have a formula sheet, which ended up only being true for the final which was a little shocking when going into the first exam. The final exam was so challenging that he did not even release the scores because he had to give such a diabolical curve due to the difficulty of the exam. This class honestly ended up testing my calculus more than my statistical skills. My main problem with this is that none of the homeworks involved calculus that was as advanced as his exams, so i didn't know I needed to brush up on things relating to calc until i was actually taking the final.

This will be an overall holistic review of his teaching through his first semester and some lore n stuff, this is gonna be fun to write. Week 1 - 3: (Intro stuff, basic set theory, combinations, counting (discrete math stuff), geometric probability, sigma algebra (wtf is that), conditional probability, and some Bayes theorem) Kinda bad, didn't have good examples through class, but wasn't the worst professor ever, started to record lectures with Notability, used the microphone (so ppl in the back of the room can hear him), and has PDFs of the notes after 2-3 weeks. Pretty rough start, but we got there. Week 4 - 6 (First Exam on Week 6) (More Bayes, Random Variables, pmf + cdf for discrete RV's, variance, binomial, geometric, and hypergeometric) Up to this point was decent, I learnt way more through the textbook and Khan Academy and videos, so yeah, also this is when I stopped going to class for a bit (all the career fair stuff was happening n shit) (The practice problems for exam 1 was basically just a random document with 50 easy questions shoved in right the Monday before the exam, just went through what we learnt up to the point) Week 7 - 10 (Fall break + negative binomial, poisson, continuous RVs, pdf, cdf, expected values, and variance. Normal + standard normal distributions, transformation of RV, exponential + gamma + weibull + log normal + gamma distribution) This week we had to go to the hotel cuz idk wtf the be doing in the Armory. This is when he turned around and became the GOAT, went to his office hours a lot between exam 1 and 2 to ask for him to help us a bit with either doing practice problems, bonus points, practice, or (Telling us exactly what type of questions would be). He said he'll think about it. We also signed a petition to ask for like either it was like bonus points or smt or like a review. And he was like (damn these kids lmao, but he did help us tho). The Tuesday right before exam 2, he said, "You have 5 questions. These are the topics to study up on, kiddos." It definitely helped me, which was nice. There was like 1 problem on negative hypergeometric (we didn't learn it, but we had to try and derive it; most ppl got 13/20 on it was super lenient on grading). Also, week 10, he started to do bonus points on the HW yeaaaa!!! Week (11 - 13): (Joint RV, Joint pdf, marginal pdf, conditional pdf, expected values, covariance and correlation, some intro statistic stuff, point estimation, methods of point estimation (Method of Moments) He started to do curves this week (thank the heavens). Other than that, this week was pretty chill. The examples were at least starting to get better, and he kinda explained like why they mattered and what it kinda means in the bigger picture. We also had the opportunity for a 2.5% grade bonus in class on the Tuesday before Thanksgiving Break. (Curve method linear transformation with the average score and max score if avg less than 83, curve up) Exam 1 Cuve = new score = 0.708 x old score + 29.17 Exam 2 Curve = new score = 0.7831 x old score + 21.69 Final Exam Curve (Maybe if we get a lower than 83% class average) Week (14 - 15) (Hypothesis testing, linear regression, MLE estimator) Slower week, was nothing crazy, we got the practice for the final 2 weeks before the final Sunday at 12:34 am, I also emailed him at 3:00 pm Sunday and said he would give it after he got home, my ass was waiting for him to post it. TAs kinda did the funny with the quizzes, and the professor had to fix their problems with not creating the same quizzes n shit, but yea We'll see for the final exam and if any new things happen, but he said that he would make the final exam easier and only 8 problems, so it should be good (I hope) Exam 1 Stats 76 avg and the highest grade was 100 Exam 2 Stats 78.29 avg and the highest grade was 100 So, not the worst and definitely got better, I guess if you can self-teach yourself first, then read his lecture recording/notes after you understand the topic, then you can ask some questions and maybe pick out some more things better in the lecture. I think he's pretty chill and yeah :thumbs_up: Hopes this helps anyone for Spring 2025 or any future semesters of STAT400 he teaches, and I hope he continues to enjoy teaching and being nice to his students Don't wanna glaze him or hate on him too much, but I think a 3 or 4 is a good rating for him.

Some things to note about the other reviews for this class, most of them were made after the first midterm. which was quite difficult and had a pretty low average. After some discussing, Professor Zheng agreed to add a curve to all the exams and the final and it was a pretty nice curve too. He also made exam 2 much easier than exam 1 and gave much better practice material, so most of these reviews are complaining about an un-curved, difficult, ill-prepared exam you won't get. About the class itself, the homework is quite difficult, much much harder than the exams or anything else you will see in class except a few examples. Professor Zheng gives out a lot of bonus points on the homework however, which makes getting a 100% in the homework category quite easy. There are weekly quizzes in discussion written by the TA's, but these are very easy compared to everything else. The review guides are pretty comprehensive and covered everything on exam 2. We did not finish all the content in the class (didn't make it to one topic) but covered almost everything so you'll still learn. I also think this class should definitely have MATH240 as a prereq, but the professor nerfed all the joint-pdf questions so knowledge from that class isn't required. Overall, STAT400 is a difficult class but Professor Zheng did a good job (in the second half of the semester) of teaching and preparing you for the exams.
