Take most of the reviews before the middle of November or with a W with a grain of salt, as these are from before the curve and massive improvement in Professor Zheng's teaching and preparation material.

I want to preface by saying she is a very nice lady and actually does want her students to do well. However, I don't recommend taking her. The material is honestly covered very fast in the lecture, she gives a lot of examples with the work, but notes are all over the place, the lecture is difficult to understand. For the calculus related part of the course she explained things well but everything related to statistics and theory was rough. Her handwriting is not great which makes it hard to write down the notes. She will answer questions thoroughly if you ask her after the lecture and that can be helpful. Exams are worth 92% of the grade, it's a 400 level class so this might be a given but the exams (at least for me) required A LOT of studying, more than all my other classes put together. There are some practice exams but answer keys are incomplete and very disorganized. The discussions are very lackluster and not helpful. It's required because you have to turn in your homework in person which is annoying. The TA will spend 15 minutes writing down the previous week's homework answers on the board instead of just posting them on canvas and taking questions in office hours. This would take away a lot of time from asking questions about the current week's material and we would almost never get through the worksheet that is supposed to be the focus of the discussion. To add on, the other professor teaching this semester gave a cheat sheet on exams and allowed calculators, our class had neither of those. There are a bunch of formulas to memorize. The only way to do well in this course unless you are just gifted at statistics is to lock in on the textbook. Do as many textbook problems as you can and you will do good on the exam. Overall, I recommend taking the class with a different professor. The grade cutoffs were very generous though.

he's clearly very knowledgeable but is a bad lecturer and definitely not approachable if you have questions. if you want to learn the material properly with clear examples, there is a full youtube course from IIT KANPUR which teaches the concepts in a more concise and understandable manner. Wish i found it on the first day - this prof lacks empathy.

Professor Richard, who looks slightly vicious, is a lovely person who is highly knowledgeable in optimization, linear algebra, calculus, and probability theory. You may sometimes see him telling jokes with no expression during the lecture, and he is very patient with every student when you ask questions during office hours, class breaks, and class. However, I admit that some course concepts are hard to comprehend at first glance. Still, after reviewing the in-class material and combining it with the external video associated with that particular concept, the difficulty of the homework should be acceptable. As for the midterm and final, he will provide some review material and the review section. If you follow the pattern of the review materials, combined with some additional practice in linear algebra, both exams are good to go. Furthermore, for the final project, I recommend consolidating the topic and working on the solution as soon as possible; it may take longer than you expected. Professor Richard has the best mathematic expertise I have ever encountered at the University of Maryland. Eventually, he will automatically and dynamically adjust the grade scope according to the entire class's performance, so do not worry too much. As the final reminder, he will review the concept of linear algebra in optimization during the first several lectures; if you are not familiar with the relative concept, please try to catch it, which will make the rest of the lecture manageable.

He had to handle a difficult topic for a class which was honestly not smart (including me). He tried his best, but it wasnt easy because of the online lectures. Class averages were pretty low throughout, but he curved generously in the end.

really nice dude grading wise (replaced our final with our project grade), all material is pretty high-level and he doesn't ask anything outside of whats in the slides. he gives you the answer pretty much for the in-class check ins and is generally very approachable.

Don't expect to learn anything, the course content is Python101. Don't trust a word he says about what will be on the exam, every one of them has at least one "gotchya" question that, at times, requires explicit quoting from the slides (not kidding). Your best bet is to read the slides if you want, but be absolutely sure to print every single one of them out for the open-book exams, because any other notes you bring will be worthless. The only A- I received in the program, not because I lacked the conceptual knowledge, but because I didn't have the slides on-hand to quote from, and I trusted him when he said we didn't need to regurgitate them. Don't get got. Print the slides.
